title: From Corporate Executive to Entrepreneur: Lyndsay Dowd's Guide to Reinvention
guestBio: Lyndsay Dowd is a globally recognized speaker, founder of Heartbeat for Hire, two-time author, and culture-shifting coach who helps companies transform toxic leadership and build irresistible workplaces. After 23 years at IBM, she made the bold leap from corporate executive to successful entrepreneur, proving that reinvention can happen at any age.
excerpt: After 23 years at IBM, Lyndsay Dowd made the courageous leap from corporate executive to entrepreneur, proving that career reinvention is possible at any stage of life. Her insights on building career security over job security will transform how you think about professional growth and personal branding.
metaDescription: Discover how Lyndsay Dowd transformed from IBM executive to successful entrepreneur, sharing strategies for career security and workplace culture transformation.
keyTakeaways: Career security is more valuable than job security in today's rapidly changing workplace landscape.
Personal branding is a leadership skill, not a luxury, and essential for professional growth at any level.
Transforming company culture from the inside out requires courage and strategic action.
Community and genuine connections are the real fuel for both happiness and professional success.
Reinvention can happen at any age or career stage with the right mindset and support system.
Building an irresistible workplace starts with ditching toxic leadership patterns and leading with heart.
The transition from corporate executive to entrepreneur requires embracing uncertainty while leveraging existing skills.

pullQuotes.text: The secret to lasting success isn't job security — it's career security

content:
What if everything you've been told about career success has been backwards? What if the real secret isn't climbing the corporate ladder or securing that corner office, but something entirely different? In this powerful episode of The Manage Her, we dive deep into a conversation that will challenge everything you think you know about professional growth, leadership, and the courage to reinvent yourself.

Lyndsay Dowd's story is one that will resonate with every woman who has ever felt the pull between security and possibility. After spending 23 years building a successful career at IBM, she made a decision that many would consider risky, even reckless. She walked away from corporate security to build something entirely her own. Today, as the founder of Heartbeat for Hire and host of an award-winning podcast that ranks in the top 2.5% globally, Lyndsay has proven that reinvention isn't just possible—it's powerful.

The Career Security Revolution

One of the most transformative concepts Lyndsay shares is the difference between job security and career security. While job security focuses on maintaining your current position, career security is about building skills, relationships, and a reputation that transcends any single role or company. This shift in thinking changes everything about how you approach your professional development.

Career security means you're not dependent on one employer, one industry, or even one career path. It means you've built a foundation of expertise, connections, and personal brand that travels with you wherever you go. You become the CEO of your own career, making strategic decisions based on growth, fulfillment, and long-term vision rather than fear of change.

This mindset becomes especially crucial in today's rapidly evolving workplace. Companies restructure, industries transform, and entire career paths can shift seemingly overnight. The women who thrive aren't those who cling most tightly to what was, but those who build the skills and confidence to navigate what's coming next.

Personal Branding as Leadership Development

Lyndsay makes a compelling case that personal branding isn't vanity—it's strategy. Personal branding is fundamentally about clarity: knowing who you are, what you stand for, and how you create value. When you're clear on these elements, every interaction, every project, and every opportunity becomes aligned with your bigger vision.

Many women struggle with personal branding because they've been conditioned to believe that self-promotion is somehow inappropriate or unprofessional. But here's the truth: if you don't define your brand, others will define it for you. And their definition might not align with your goals, your values, or your potential.

Building your personal brand means becoming intentional about how you show up. It means taking ownership of your narrative and ensuring that your reputation reflects your capabilities and aspirations. This isn't about becoming someone you're not—it's about becoming more fully who you are and communicating that effectively to the world.

Transforming Culture from Within

Before starting her own company, Lyndsay spent years working to transform culture from within large organizations. Her experience offers valuable insights for any woman trying to create change in her current workplace. Culture transformation doesn't always require a title or formal authority—it requires courage and consistency.

The most effective culture change often happens through small, daily actions. It's about modeling the behavior you want to see, having difficult conversations when necessary, and consistently advocating for better ways of working. Sometimes it means being willing to be uncomfortable in service of creating a more inclusive, supportive environment for everyone.

Lyndsay's work in helping companies build "irresistible workplaces" starts with addressing toxic leadership patterns. This means challenging behaviors that undermine trust, communication, and collaboration. It means creating spaces where people can bring their full selves to work and where diverse perspectives are not just welcomed but actively sought.

The Power of Community and Connection

Throughout her journey from corporate executive to entrepreneur, Lyndsay discovered that community and connection are the real fuel for both happiness and success. This insight challenges the myth of the self-made individual and acknowledges the reality that our biggest achievements happen in relationship with others.

Building meaningful professional relationships isn't about networking in the traditional sense—it's about genuine connection. It's about being curious about others, offering help before you need it, and creating value in your interactions. These relationships become the foundation for opportunities, collaborations, and support during challenging times.

Community also provides something that individual achievement cannot: perspective. When you're surrounded by other ambitious, thoughtful people, you're constantly challenged to grow, to think differently, and to expand your vision of what's possible. You see examples of paths you might not have considered and gain the confidence to take risks you might not have taken alone.

Embracing Reinvention at Any Stage

Perhaps the most powerful message in Lyndsay's story is that reinvention can happen at any age, in any season of life. The narrative that major career changes are only for twenty-somethings is not only false—it's limiting. Experience, wisdom, and established networks can actually be significant advantages when making a career transition.

Reinvention doesn't necessarily mean starting from scratch. It means taking everything you've learned, all the skills you've developed, and all the relationships you've built, and applying them in a new context. Your corporate experience doesn't become irrelevant when you become an entrepreneur—it becomes your competitive advantage.

The key is approaching reinvention with both courage and strategy. It means being honest about what's not working in your current situation while being realistic about what it will take to create something better. It means building toward your new vision while maintaining the responsibilities you currently have.

Lyndsay's journey from IBM executive to successful entrepreneur and podcast host demonstrates that when you align your work with your values and leverage your unique combination of skills and experience, you don't just build a career—you build a legacy. Her story is permission for every woman listening to consider what might be possible if you stopped playing it safe and started playing to win.

quiz.title: What's Your Reinvention Style?
quiz.description: Ready to discover how you approach change and transformation in your career? This quiz reveals your unique reinvention style based on Lyndsay Dowd's powerful journey from corporate executive to entrepreneur.

questions.question: When you think about your next career move, what drives you most?

options.text: Creating a detailed plan that minimizes risk while maximizing opportunity
Finding work that aligns with my values and makes me excited to get up each day
Building relationships and finding ways to collaborate with inspiring people
Developing skills that will serve me well no matter how the industry changes

questions.question: Your dream job posting appears, but it requires skills you don't have yet. Your first thought is:

options.text: I need to create a learning plan and timeline to acquire these skills systematically
If this role calls to me, I'll figure out how to make it work as I go
I should reach out to people in my network who can help me learn or get connected
This is exactly the kind of stretch opportunity that builds career security

questions.question: When facing a major career decision, you typically:

options.text: Make pros and cons lists, research extensively, and create contingency plans
Pay attention to how each option makes you feel and what your intuition says
Talk it through with trusted mentors, friends, and colleagues
Consider how each path builds your long-term professional foundation

questions.question: What scares you most about making a big career change?

options.text: Not having enough information or preparation before taking the leap
Compromising my values or losing sight of what matters most to me
Losing the professional relationships and community I've built
Giving up stability without knowing if the new path will provide security

questions.question: Your ideal work environment is one where:

options.text: There are clear processes, growth opportunities, and room for strategic thinking
The culture is authentic, the mission is meaningful, and leadership leads with heart
Collaboration is valued, relationships matter, and everyone supports each other
You can build expertise, develop new skills, and create lasting professional value

questions.question: When you imagine yourself in five years, you see:

options.text: Someone who has executed a well-thought-out plan and achieved specific goals
Someone doing work that feels authentic and makes a meaningful impact
Someone surrounded by an incredible community of colleagues and collaborators
Someone with a robust skill set and reputation that opens any door you want

results.title: The Strategic Transformer
results.description: You approach reinvention with careful planning and methodical steps. You're the woman who researches every angle, builds bridges before burning them, and creates detailed roadmaps for change. Your superpower is making bold moves feel manageable and sustainable. Lyndsay's episode will show you how to leverage your planning skills while embracing the uncertainty that comes with true transformation.

results.title: The Heart-Led Pioneer
results.description: You follow your intuition and values when making big life changes, often trusting your gut over conventional wisdom. You're drawn to purpose-driven work and aren't afraid to choose meaning over money. Your courage to lead with your heart is your greatest asset. This episode will resonate deeply with you as Lyndsay shares how she built an 'irresistible workplace' by ditching toxic patterns and leading authentically.

results.title: The Connection Catalyst
results.description: You understand that relationships and community are the real fuel for both happiness and professional success. You naturally build networks and see collaboration as key to growth. Your ability to create genuine connections opens doors others can't even see. Lyndsay's insights about community-building and personal branding as a leadership skill will give you powerful tools to amplify your natural networking gifts.

results.title: The Security Innovator
results.description: You're thoughtful about balancing stability with growth, always looking for ways to innovate within structure. You understand the value of building career security over job security, even if change feels scary sometimes. Your practical wisdom helps you take calculated risks that pay off long-term. This episode will validate your approach while showing you how to build the foundation for reinvention at any stage.

transcript:
**Aimee:** Let's have some fun. All right, Lindsay, it's so nice to have you on the show. I'm so excited you're here today.

**Lyndsay:** I could not be more excited for this, Aimee. Let's do this.

**Aimee:** All right, so let's start with reinvention at 50. You started Heartbeat for Hire at 50 after more than two decades at IBM. So, what gave you the courage to step away from corporate life and build something of your own?

**Lyndsay:** I don't know that I knew it was courage at the time. When I left IBM and I went to this other company and they fired me after six months, it was such a blow. I thought I was the only executive to ever be fired on Earth. I thought no one would ever work with me again. I thought I had a scarlet letter and I was at a real loss. 

So, after a month of licking my wounds, I asked myself three questions. What am I really good at? What do I love to do? And how can I help people the most? And that's what gave me my true north and my compass for what kind of work I wanted to do. And I was so burned by that experience that gave me a lot of fire to move forward and create Heartbeat for Hire. So, I don't know if it was bravery or it was determination or if it was just blind brass balls, but I just went for it.

**Aimee:** It takes more than courage sometimes to do what we need to do, you know, to make shifts. Big shifts. Sometimes you get thrown into big shifts in life. I know most of my big shifts in life have not been something I was seeking, but something that happened to me. And it's like, all right, how can I grow from this?

**Lyndsay:** What are we going to do, right? I also don't think I knew what the hell I was doing either. And I thought this glorious 25-year career of doing hard things and creating first-in-class businesses and managing big accounts would have me very well equipped for this next chapter. Wrong. It did not work that way at all. I learned so much. I learned so many lessons and entrepreneurship is very different than working in a corporate setting. And I'm sure you know that.

**Aimee:** It's so different. Yeah. I think when you're at a corporation, it's a little easier to, I don't know, almost hide. Your work is a little more obscured.

**Lyndsay:** Lots of people around. But when you're on your own, there's no one else. And if you don't do it, no one else is going to do it.

**Aimee:** There were so many things I didn't think about like I have to do taxes.

**Lyndsay:** Yes.

**Aimee:** I have to plan my own travel. There was all these things that I was like, "Oh, there's no one picking up this slack. This is just all mine now." So, you know, it had pros and cons.

**Lyndsay:** I do love that Expedia app though. That's how I book the corporate travel for the company. Everyone's information is just loaded and I can just go.

**Aimee:** Yeah, there you go. Perfect. So, you've seen leadership done both brilliantly and badly. What made you want to dedicate this chapter of your life to transforming company culture?

**Lyndsay:** I mean, the most recent example of what I had to experience was enough to make me not want people to have to experience that kind of leadership again. And when I keynote and I ask a room full of people, who here has had a bad leader? 100% raise their hand. 100%. And then when I say who here has had a great leader, it's a lot less. And that was just fuel for me to say no, we can do better than that.

And how I learn is through stories. And that's how I speak. That's how I teach. That's how I sold. That's how I managed my teams. And so I was focused on how can I leverage story to help people understand what bad leadership looks and feels like and what good leadership is supposed to be. And if you've never had a good leader, you don't know what that looks like. And I was one of those people for the first 15 years of my career. All of my leaders were pale male and stale dudes in tech who gave these sports analogies as motivation. "The game last night on the 30 yard line, this should make you want to sell." I'm like this should make me want to leave. This is not inspiring. I can't stand these analogies and I didn't watch the game and I'm not going to watch the game. 

And so all that stuff just felt so inauthentic and unrealistic. And I was just this is lame. And then when you get your first great leader who sees you and understands what you're capable of and says these things to you "Okay, Lindsay, I just promoted you. How do you want to run the business?" Me? You want to know what I think? "Yeah. You've been doing this for a while. So, how do you want to do it?" Oh my gosh, when these things happen, it's groundbreaking. It's this is how it's supposed to be. And so, I collected those stories from my own experience and from the hundreds and hundreds of leaders I've worked with in the last three years that show the right way. And there's tons of stories on how to do it right.

**Aimee:** I absolutely love it. And it's such a woman's way. You know, storytelling is such the woman's way to do things. And it's just a great way to teach humanity. It's why the Bible was such a popular thing. It doesn't it's not just a list of rules. It's a bunch of stories that show you the parables of things. And that's really the way that humanity has learned over and over and over again. I love that. So, you just get up there and tell stories and help people change the way that they lead.

**Lyndsay:** So, when I keynote, I take people on a roller coaster. I mean, we start off and I give them a complete tragic disaster story that makes them audibly gasp and these are all true and they're "Oh." And then I give them a really good story and we take them up and then we go crashing down for another terrible one and then a good one and then a crash again. But those crashing stories are so abhorrent and so terrible that they remember those and they remember the good ones too because the good ones also yielded results and I closed some of my biggest deals under my greatest leaders.

And that's the point. It's not just let's kumbaya and have a good time. It's let's change the ROI. Let's change the energy and the feeling of this company. And when your people are excited about what they're doing, they produce. It's that simple. And there's been a million studies that show this from Harvard, from MIT, from everywhere that I've seen that I've included in my book. I mean, this is not Lindsay just telling you this is right. This is proven. And I just hope that more leaders sign on to the leading with heart style of leadership because it works.

**Aimee:** I absolutely love it. I mean, I love it because that's how I to lead, too. I really want everybody to feel super stoked about what they're doing. I want them to feel they're doing what they want to be doing. They feel capable doing what I've asked them to do and they're excited about it.

**Lyndsay:** Feel seen. And there's, you know, so many leaders succumb to the pressure of I've got a quota. I've got to get this deadline. I've got whatever that is. and they lose track of the fact that they're responsible to their people. And when you think about the moment you become a manager, you become the topic of dinner conversation. So make sure it's good because the reason people leave and when you ask somebody, how do you your job? They're answering how they feel about their boss. So the bosses are the reason that people leave. It's not usually the company.

**Aimee:** So true. It always comes down to people. Yeah. People create the culture, too.

**Lyndsay:** Yeah. People are the culture.

**Aimee:** I love this. We were talking about this. You said there's no more job security anymore. Only career security. What does that mean? And how can people build it through their personal brand and network?

**Lyndsay:** Yeah. I mean, well, think about it this way. There is nobody that does what I did. There's no one staying at jobs for 20 years anymore. That just rarely happens. And if they're there, it's just because they've been there and they never had the courage to leave or they have such a sweet deal that they're not going anywhere. So, it's normal to bounce around. But it's also normal to lose your job. And that is a thing that certainly I didn't know when I lost my job because I never experienced anything that before. But it is 100% normal to expect layoffs, to expect downsizing, to expect, you know, personality conflicts and people just get moved out of jobs.

So if you're going to lose your job and if you can't depend on I'm going to be here until I retire, that's nobody can say that anymore with much confidence. So, you have to build your personal brand because once you lose that job or that title or that network, what do you got? You have this resume, but you've done nothing with it and nobody knows who you are. And if you've made the same mistake that I made back in the day, you know, I had all these people that were reaching out to me on LinkedIn, but I'm "you don't work for IBM. You're not my customer. You're not my business partner. Don't know you. Bye." What a dumb way to leverage my network. All I needed to do was be "Oh, that person's interesting. Maybe I could learn from them." 

So, I teach how to use LinkedIn to build your brand. And LinkedIn is no longer just an online resume. This is a platform for expertise. And so, thought leaders show up there. And the really interesting part about LinkedIn, a lot of people when I'm first coaching them on LinkedIn, they're "Oh my god, are you gonna make me be a content creator?" Because I don't have that kind of bandwidth. I don't want to make a post. I don't want to do videos. I don't want to post selfies. No, that's not how you grow anyway. How you grow on LinkedIn is in the comments. 

And so if you sit there and Aimee does this great post on leadership or let's say she does a great post on leadership. Well, I go out to Aimee's and I'm "Aimee, I love how you said this. In fact, I'm going to double down on this part and I'm going to say this." And what's going to happen is Aimee's Network is going to be "Who's that Lindsay lady? She's got something to say. I want to be connected to her." And so all of a sudden, now, by the way, LinkedIn is giving you analytics on your comments, not just your posts. So, it's telling you how many people saw that comment. I'm telling you, I have hundreds, if not thousands of people seeing my comments every day, and these are not my posts. These are just comments. That's how you grow.

**Aimee:** Wow, Lindsay. That's amazing.

**Lyndsay:** It's cool.

**Aimee:** Wow.

**Lyndsay:** When you think about how you show up, you're not gonna write vice president of business development and call it a day. You're gonna write a few things that you are. I'm a multi-hyphenate. So mine now, I mean the last three years, I'm a podcast host, an author, a speaker, a coach, a founder. Yeah, all of those things, but I'm other things, too. And I always encourage people to put some personal stuff in there. So mine also says mom of twins and wiener dog lover. Shout out to Poppy Long Sausage, my eight pound wonder who's down here. 

The reason you do the personal thing is because it's a softball. It's an easy way to give someone a reason to connect. And the amount of people that comment on the wiener dog lover or on the twins thing is remarkable. So "oh, you have twins? I'm a twin. Or my brother's a twin or I have twins." You know, it's just an easy way to connect. So I will tell you compared to all of the social platforms and the only one I'm not on is X. This is the most engaged, the friendliest, the most curious, and if you are trying to make a career at whatever it is, even if it's your side hustle, you want to highlight, that's the place you want to do it.

**Aimee:** I sound I work for LinkedIn. I really don't. And they did not sponsor this podcast.

**Lyndsay:** I'm telling you.

**Aimee:** I love it. I love it, though. You go. This is how you no, you're so right. I never even thought about LinkedIn that way at all. You know, it was just sort of for me it was something that I had to have as I was going out and getting these, you know, larger corporate contracts and it was sort of a okay well you have to do this and okay well we'll do this. So it's just a business card.

**Lyndsay:** Let me blow your mind with one more thing. Executives who post get something 360% more reach than a regular contributor and your brand becomes 82% more trustworthy because you are an executive posting. So any C-suite executive that's "I have time for LinkedIn I have people to manage" stop right now get out there and start talking. 

I was coaching a CEO last year and she had gone from CFO to CEO and she hired me because she's "you know, this is a different voice." And I'm "oh yeah, that's a really different voice to go from CFO to CEO." She goes, I said, so why do you want to work with me? And she said, well, I do really good work and people respect my work, but I don't think they me. Oh, okay. All right. Well, we've got to humanize your voice and you're going to start using LinkedIn. And she's "I have people for that." I go, no, no, no. You're going to start welcoming every new hire onto, you know, welcome to the team, so-and-so, and they're going to see the CEO of my company just welcomed me. Wow. Wow. That's really meaningful. What did that take her? Six seconds. She can do that.

**Aimee:** Yeah.

**Lyndsay:** And when someone has a big win, give them a shout out. Talk about it. Who doesn't want to work for a CEO that sees their people? It's so easy. So, that's just a super simple way for people to think about it. But when you reach out to a brand and that brand responds, you're stoked, right? How many times have we reached out to brands who never respond and you're always "So much for that." Shouting into the void again.

**Aimee:** Yeah. Yeah. So, think about it.

**Lyndsay:** Amazing. No, that's those are pearls of wisdom. I love it. You're just, you know, and you're full of those. We were talking about another example of a recent college graduate that you coached and he's a second generation outlier from the outlier project which we're both members and you know how you basically mentored this kid as he's right out of college and what was the advice you gave him?

**Lyndsay:** Yeah. So Ben reached out to me his dad and I are friends and he was "my dad says you know you know everything." I'm "oh I don't know anything but go ahead." And he's "So, what advice do you have for me since I just graduated college?" And I said, "All right, I'm going to give you advice that you're probably never going to get from a college professor or any of your parents' friends, but I want you to start a side hustle." And he was "Uh, now?" And I said, "Yeah, and I'm going to tell you why. You're going to do your job search. You're going to get your job, but we said before, there's no such thing as job security. So when you always have something to fall back on that's yours, you always will have some kind of source of income. And I said, I'm going to guess that because you're young and whatever generation that is, I always screw them up. The young one, the Gen Alpha or Gen whatever, that generation knows technology, knows video, knows content, knows social media it's in their brains. Easy, easy for them. I have 17-year-old twins, so I know this to be true. And so he's "No, Linds. I'm a whiz at video. I'm a whiz at creating graphic stuff and all that kind of thing." And I'm "See, I told you." 

I go, "How about this? You create me a video for my podcast and I'm going to shout you out to all the outliers and on LinkedIn." And he's "Oh, it's on. Let's go." And so he created the most fabulous video. I was trying to get a brand deal with ColorWow. ColorWow, if you're listening, I'm still here. So would love to partner with them. But we did this really funny, clever video of a hundred different images of all my different hairdos while I'm doing my podcast and I wanted them to sponsor my podcast and it was super cute, great music. It was awesome. I was so impressed. So I shouted him out. I think he got six clients out of that. And so his side hustle is humming. And now on his LinkedIn that is part of his brand.

**Aimee:** That is so cool. And I don't think people realize especially young people I don't think they realize how easy this social media hustle is. In fact I just hired a girl. She's still in college and we helped her actually form her own company cuz I'm girl you don't just do this for me. Do this for a lot of people. I will introduce you to people and we will build you a company and you can do social media and then you can figure out whatever else you want to do with your life. But for now, this will cover your needs. This is going to cover your money. For them, it's a no-brainer. And listen, everybody in their 40s and up, we're not good at this. We don't our brains don't work that way. And it, you know, no matter how many times I create a reel I don't think my muscles are getting stronger. I think they're just stuck. So these guys have really good social muscles that way and I love to leverage them. So it's a joy to work with these guys who are so fast and I don't think

**Lyndsay:** It's they're born fluent in social media and we didn't we know we're not fluent because we didn't grow up with it. It's new for us. You know we it wasn't here.

**Aimee:** Listening go encourage your kids to do this. This is a great side hustle and I know my daughter, she's managing three social accounts for her school and she wants to go into marketing in college. So, this is excellent experience for her to go and she's not getting paid for that. So, if she were smart, she'd come work for me. But whatever, right? Eventually. Eventually, right?

**Lyndsay:** Yeah. Eventually, right? Eventually, when she needs the money, right? For sure. Well, let's talk a little bit about Outlier Project because I'm sure there's a lot of people that are unfamiliar with what the Outlier Project is. And yeah, let's get into it.

**Lyndsay:** All right, so the Outlier Project, as I love to talk about it, this is 800 or so people who choose to live extraordinary lives. And that's a nice statement and all, but I will tell you, this community has completely changed my life. And I don't just mean you join the community and you know once in a while you talk to one person. I'm saying every week we have access to an incredible legend. And I'm talking Guy Kawasaki or we just had Renee Martino on last week. She was the mother in Jersey Boys on Broadway and then went and starred in the movie Clint Eastwood's movie as the mother in Jersey Boys on that and she's become this baller coach. And it was such a joy and a thrill to listen to her. We have incredible keynote speakers, veterans, professional athletes, Dwight Evans, Dominique Wilkins, Leila Ali, and we get an hour with these people every week. So, we get to ask them anything, which is super cool. And I, the way I play it and the way I nurture those relationships, they become my friends. They end up on my show. It's pretty great. 

So aside from that though, you have all these people who are doing phenomenally cool stuff. Mix of entrepreneurs, corporate people, speakers, athletes, authors, professors, you name it. And because we are so intent on living these big lives, we vibrate at a real high level. So whenever you're in company with these people, it's not a lot of small talk. We kind of go right in. And these people have become my entire personal board of advisors. Whenever I'm doubting myself, I can make one of 50 phone calls or a text and be "I really need some advice or I'm doubting myself." And I'm usually met with, you're Lindsay Dowd. You belong in every room. Knock it off. And you know, that kind of confidence and connection is precious. And I know you know this. We've talked about this before, but when you tell your family or your childhood friends or your neighbors that you're doing, you know, your business or you're building something new, they don't know what that is. They don't know what that means. They don't understand how you're podcasting. They don't understand all this stuff. And they don't know how to support you. And they project all of their fears onto you.

**Aimee:** Yeah.

**Lyndsay:** This group doesn't do that. We're the opposite. We're "Hell yeah, double down. Let's go. I have an introduction for you." And that's a fabulous way to live. So, on top of all that, we get together every month somewhere in the country. I think there's a meetup in Miami this month. Then they're going to the Yale and Harvard game. Then they'll do ice skating in Rockefeller Center. And we do stuff on the West Coast. We do it in Midwest. And we all see each other. We travel for each other, too. So, it's a pretty special group. And oh, by the way, our mascot is the platypus because, you know, that's the ultimate outlier.
